front: Poem: opens "All I could see from where I stood / Was three long mountains and a wood"; speaker feels "Infinity / Pressed down upon the finite Me" and sinks underground before rebirth
Poem: speaker sees "A man was starving in Capri; He moved his eyes and looked at me" — later warns "he whose soul is flat, the sky will cave in on him"
Poem: "My candle burns at both ends; / It will not last the night; / But ah, my foes, and oh, my friends— / It gives a lovely light!"
Poem: commands "let all who prate of Beauty hold their peace" and describes "the terrible day, when first the shaft into his vision shone / of light anatomized!"
back: "Euclid alone has looked on Beauty bare" (Edna St. Vincent Millay)
work: Euclid alone has looked on Beauty bare

front: Poem: a mother freezes to death weaving "the clothes of a king's son" for her child using a "harp with a woman's head"
back: "The Ballad of the Harp-Weaver" (Edna St. Vincent Millay)

front: Poem: speaker compares herself to a tree that "knows its boughs more silent than before"; "the rain / Is full of ghosts tonight" and "summer sang in me / A little while"
back: "What lips my lips have kissed" (Edna St. Vincent Millay)
work: What lips my lips have kissed, and where, and why

front: Poem: "Love is not all: it is not meat nor drink / Nor slumber nor a roof against the rain" — from a collection of 52 sonnets, one per week of an affair
back: "Love is not all" from Fatal Interview (Edna St. Vincent Millay)

front: Poem: retells Bluebeard's story — the secret chamber is empty, "cobwebbed and comfortless"; ends "This now is yours. I seek another place"
Author: associated with the Provincetown Players; wrote the anti-war play Aria da Capo featuring Corydon and Thyrsis with a masque overseen by Cothurnus
Poem: repeats "We were very tired, we were very merry" as two girls ride back and forth on a ferry all night
